title: Mujaddara
subtitle: Lebanese lentils and rice with dark caramelised onions
excerpt: Brown lentils and basmati rice cooked together with warm spices, topped with onions fried very slowly in a generous amount of olive oil until they are dark, sweet, and beginning to crisp at the edges. The onions are the point of the dish. Everything else is a backdrop for them.
sourceNotes: Mujaddara is documented in the thirteenth-century Baghdadi cookbook Kitab al-Tabikh, making it one of the oldest continuously made dishes in the Arabic culinary tradition. The word means pockmarked in Arabic, referring to the lentils visible through the rice. The dish is a staple across Lebanon, Syria, and Jordan.

recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well. Keeps for 4 days in the fridge. Make the onions fresh each time.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The rice and lentil base reheats well with a splash of water. Fry the onions fresh to serve.

content.text: Mujaddara is a dish with two modest ingredients (lentils and rice) and one that transforms the whole thing: onions cooked in a great deal of olive oil until they are dark, almost catching at the edges, and collapsing into sweetness. The onions cannot be rushed. They need 35 to 40 minutes over medium heat. Starting high and trying to speed things up produces soft golden onions, which is pleasant but not the same dish.

The rice and lentils cook together in spiced water with cumin and a little cinnamon. The finished dish is served at room temperature or warm, never piping hot, with the dark onions piled on top and a spoon of yoghurt on the side if you want it.

Place the {{lentils-brown}} of lentils in a saucepan with 1 litre of cold water. Bring to the boil, skim any foam, then reduce the heat and simmer for 15 to 18 minutes until just tender but still holding their shape. Drain, reserving 450 ml of the cooking water. Set aside.

While the lentils cook, heat the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide frying pan over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} sliced onions with a large pinch of salt and cook, stirring every 5 minutes, for 35 to 40 minutes until very dark brown with crispy edges. The colour should be deeper than you think is right — aim for mahogany, not gold. Set aside half the onions; keep the rest in the pan.
Cook the rice and lentils together

Add the {{cumin-ground}} of cumin and {{cinnamon-ground}} of cinnamon to the pan of remaining onions. Stir for 1 minute. Add the rinsed {{basmati-rice}} of rice, the par-cooked lentils, and the reserved 450 ml of cooking water. Season with the {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and stir to combine. Bring to the boil, then cover tightly and cook over the lowest possible heat for 15 minutes. Remove from the heat and rest, covered, for 10 minutes without lifting the lid. Fluff with a fork. Serve topped with the reserved crispy onions and {{greek-yoghurt}} Greek yoghurt alongside.

items.symptom: Onions are golden but not crispy or dark
items.cause: Cooked at too high a temperature and not for long enough
items.fix: Keep the heat at medium; 35 to 40 minutes is not optional — golden onions are fine but the dish needs the bitter-sweet intensity of dark caramelisation

items.symptom: Rice is undercooked or crunchy in the centre
items.cause: Not enough liquid, or lid lifted during resting
items.fix: Add a splash of boiling water and cook covered for 5 more minutes; keep the lid on during the 10-minute rest

items.symptom: Dish is underseasoned despite correct quantities
items.fix: Season the cooking water well before adding the rice and lentils; the starch absorbs salt as it cooks

content.text: Mujaddara appears in the thirteenth-century Baghdadi cookbook Kitab al-Tabikh, making it one of the longest-running dishes in Arabic cuisine. Across Lebanon, Syria, and Jordan it is everyday home cooking — the sort of thing made when the fridge is nearly empty. There is a story that Esau sold his birthright for a dish of lentils and rice; if so, it was probably mujaddara. The crispy onions are not a garnish: they are the flavour that holds the whole thing together.
